##### 3.3.1.4. Liaisons between the Advisory Board and the Board of Directors[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ab-bod-liaison)

To ensure good communication between the [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 and the [Board of Directors](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#bod)
 and facilitate operational and management consistency, the [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 _may_ appoint up to two of its participants as liaisons to the [Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#bod)
. Such appointees are expected to attend and participate in [Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#bod)
 meetings and access [Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#bod)
 materials as Non-voting Observers. [\[BYLAWS\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-bylaws "Amended and Restated Bylaws of World Wide Web Consortium, Inc.")
 They do not form part of the [Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#bod)
's decision-making body, and may be excluded from such participation in accordance with applicable Board procedures.

The [Advisory Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 _should_ reevaluate who is assigned to this role at least at the beginning of each term, and _may_ swap its appointees more frequently as they deem appropriate.

#### 3.3.2. Technical Architecture Group (TAG)[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TAG)

##### 3.3.2.1. Role of the Technical Architecture Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#tag-role)

The mission of the TAG is stewardship of the Web architecture. There are three aspects to this mission:

1.  to document and build consensus around principles of Web architecture and to interpret and clarify these principles when necessary;
2.  to resolve issues involving general Web architecture brought to the TAG;
3.  to help coordinate cross-technology architecture developments inside and outside W3C.

As part of a [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
, the members of the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 hear and adjudicate on [Submission Appeals](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#SubmissionNo)
 and [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
.

The [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
's scope is limited to technical issues about Web architecture. The TAG _should not_ consider administrative, process, or organizational policy issues of W3C, which are generally addressed by the W3C Advisory Committee, Advisory Board, and Team. Please refer to the [TAG charter](https://www.w3.org/2004/10/27-tag-charter.html)
 [\[TAG-CHARTER\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-tag-charter "Technical Architecture Group (TAG) Charter")
 for more information about the background and scope of the TAG, and the expected qualifications of TAG participants.

When the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 votes to resolve an issue, each TAG participant (whether appointed, elected, or the Chair) has one vote; see also the section on [voting in the TAG charter](https://www.w3.org/2004/10/27-tag-charter.html#Voting)
 [\[TAG-CHARTER\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-tag-charter "Technical Architecture Group (TAG) Charter")
 and the general section on [votes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Votes)
 in this Process Document.

Details about the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 (e.g., the list of TAG participants, mailing list information, and summaries of TAG meetings) are available at the [TAG home page](https://www.w3.org/2001/tag/)
 [\[TAG-HP\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-tag-hp "The TAG home page")
.

##### 3.3.2.2. Composition of the Technical Architecture Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#tag-participation)

The [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 consists of:

*   Tim Berners-Lee, who is a life member;
*   Three participants [appointed by the Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TAG-appointments)
    ;
*   Eight participants elected by the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
     following the [AB/TAG nomination and election process](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-elections)
    .

Participants in the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 choose by consensus their Chair or co-Chairs; in the absence of consensus, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 appoints the Chair or co-Chairs of the TAG. The Chair or co-Chairs _must_ be selected from the participants of the TAG. Chair selection _must_ be run at least at the start of each regular term, as well as when a majority of the participants request it; and _may_ be run at other times when initiated by the current chairs or the Team, for example if a chair steps down or if a minority of the participants make such a request.

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 also appoints a [Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/Guide/teamcontact/role.html)
 [\[TEAM-CONTACT\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-team-contact "Role of the Team Contact")
 for the TAG, as described in [§ 3.4.1 Requirements for All Chartered Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ReqsAllGroups)
.

The terms of TAG participants last for two years. Terms are staggered so that four elected terms and either one or two appointed terms expire each year. If an individual is appointed or elected to fill an incomplete term, that individual’s term ends at the normal expiration date of that term. Regular TAG terms begin on 1 February and end on 31 January.

##### 3.3.2.3. Communications of the Technical Architecture Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#tag-comm)

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ make available two mailing lists for the TAG:

*   a public discussion (not just input) list for issues of Web architecture. The [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
     will conduct its public business on this list.
*   a [Member-only](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Member-only)
     list for discussions within the TAG and for requests to the TAG that, for whatever reason, cannot be made on the public list.

The [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 _may_ also request the creation of additional topic-specific, public mailing lists. For some TAG discussions (e.g., a [Submission Appeal](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#SubmissionNo)
), the TAG _may_ use a list that will be [Member-only](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Member-only)
.

The [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 _should_ send a summary of each of its [meetings](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralMeetings)
 to the Advisory Committee and other group Chairs. The [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 _should_ also report on its activities at each [Advisory Committee meeting](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACMeetings)
.

#### 3.3.3. Participation in Elected Groups[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-participation)

##### 3.3.3.1. Expectations for Elected Groups Participants[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#elected-expectations)

[Advisory Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 and [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 participants have a special role within W3C: they are elected by the Membership and appointed by the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 with the expectation that they will use their best judgment to find the best solutions for the Web, not just for any particular network, technology, vendor, or user. Advisory Board and TAG participants are expected to participate regularly and fully. Advisory Board and TAG participants _should_ attend [Advisory Committee meetings](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACMeetings)
.

Individuals elected or appointed to the Advisory Board or TAG act in their personal capacity, to serve the needs of the W3C membership as a whole, and the Web community. Whether they are Member representatives or Invited Experts, their activities in those roles are separate and distinct from their activities on the Advisory Board or TAG.

An individual participates on the Advisory Board or TAG from the moment the individual’s term begins until the seat is [vacated](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-vacated)
 (e.g. because the term ends). Although Advisory Board and TAG participants do not advocate for the commercial interests of their employers, their participation does carry the responsibilities associated with Member representation, Invited Expert status, or Team representation (as described in the section on the [AB/TAG nomination and election process](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-elections)
).

Participation in the TAG or AB is afforded to the specific individuals elected or appointed to those positions, and a participant’s seat _must not_ be delegated to any other person.

##### 3.3.3.2. Elected Groups Participation Constraints[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-constraints)

Given the few seats available on the [Advisory Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 and the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
, and in order to ensure that the diversity of W3C Members is represented:

*   Two participants with the same [primary affiliation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#primary-affiliation)
     per [§ 3.3.3.3 Advisory Board and Technical Architecture Group Elections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-elections)
     must not both occupy elected seats on the TAG except when this is caused by a change of affiliation of an existing participant. At the completion of the next regularly scheduled election for the TAG, the organization _must_ have returned to having at most one seat.
*   Two participants with the same [primary affiliation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#primary-affiliation)
     per [§ 3.3.3.3 Advisory Board and Technical Architecture Group Elections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-elections)
     must not both occupy elected seats on the AB. If, for whatever reason, these constraints are not satisfied (e.g., because an [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
     participant changes jobs), one participant _must_ cease [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
     participation until the situation has been resolved. If after 30 days the situation has not been resolved, the Chair will apply the [verifiable random selection procedure](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#random)
     described below to choose one person for continued participation and declare the other seat(s) vacant.
*   An individual _must not_ participate on both the TAG and the AB.

##### 3.3.3.3. Advisory Board and Technical Architecture Group Elections[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-elections)

The [Advisory Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 and a portion of the [Technical Architecture Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 are elected by the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
, using a Single Transferable Vote system. An election begins when the Team sends a Call for Nominations to the Advisory Committee. Any Call for Nominations specifies the minimum and maximum number of available seats, the deadline for nominations, details about the specific vote tabulation system selected by the Team for the election, and operational information such as how to nominate a candidate. The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _may_ modify the tabulation system after the Call for Nominations but _must_ stabilize it no later than the Call for Votes. The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 announces appointments after the results of the election are known, and before the start of the term, as described in [§ 3.3.3.4 Technical Architecture Group Appointments](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TAG-appointments)
.

In the case of regularly scheduled elections of the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
, the minimum and maximum number of available seats are the same: the 4 seats of the terms expiring that year, plus the number of other seats that are vacant or will be vacant by the time the newly elected members take their seats.

In the case of regularly scheduled elections of the [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
, the minimum and maximum number of available seats differ: The maximum number is the 5 or 6 seats of the terms expiring that year, plus the number of other seats that are vacant or will be vacant by the time the newly elected members take their seats; the minimum number is such that when added to the occupied seats from the prior year, the minimum size of the AB (9) is reached.

Each Member (or group of [related Members](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#related-member)
) _may_ nominate one individual. A nomination _must_ be made with the consent of the nominee. In order for an individual to be nominated as a Member representative, the individual _must_ qualify for [Member representation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-rep)
 and the Member’s [Advisory Committee representative](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
 _must_ include in the nomination the (same) [information required for a Member representative in a Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-rep-info)
. In order for an individual to be nominated as an Invited Expert, the individual _must_ provide the (same) [information required for an Invited Expert in a Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#inv-expert-info)
 and the nominating [Advisory Committee representative](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
 _must_ include that information in the nomination. In order for an individual to be nominated as a [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 representative, the nominating [Advisory Committee representative](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
 _must_ first secure approval from [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 management. A nominee is _not required_ to be an employee of a Member organization, and _may_ be a [W3C Fellow](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#fellows)
. The nomination form _must_ ask for the nominee’s [primary affiliation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#primary-affiliation)
, and this will be reported on the ballot. For most nominees, the primary affiliation is their employer and will match their affiliation in the W3C database. For contractors and invited experts, this will normally be their contracting company or their invited expert status; in some cases (e.g. where a consultant is consulting for only one organization) this _may_ be the organization for whom the nominee is consulting. A [change of affiliation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-constraints)
 is defined such that this field would carry a different answer if the nominee were to be re-nominated (therefore, terminating employment, or accepting new employment, are changes of affiliation). (Other formal relationships such as other contracts should be disclosed as potential conflicts of interest.) Each nomination _should_ include a few informative paragraphs about the nominee.

If, after the deadline for nominations, the number of nominees is:

*   Greater than or equal to the minimum number of available seats and less than or equal to the maximum number of available seats, those nominees are thereby elected. This situation constitutes a tie for the purpose of assigning [incomplete terms](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#short-term)
    . Furthermore, if the number is less than the maximum number of available seats, the longest terms are filled first.
*   Less than the minimum number of available seats, Calls for Nominations are issued until a sufficient number of people have been nominated. Those already nominated do not need to be renominated after a renewed call.
*   Greater than the maximum number of available seats, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
     issues a Call for Votes that includes the names of all candidates, the (maximum) number of available seats, the deadline for votes, details about the vote tabulation system selected by the Team for the election, and operational information.

When there is a vote, each Member (or group of [related Members](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#related-member)
) _may_ submit one ballot that ranks candidates in the Member’s preferred order. Once the deadline for votes has passed, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 announces the results to the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
. In case of a tie the [verifiable random selection procedure](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#random)
 described below will be used to fill the available seats.

The shortest incomplete term is assigned to the elected candidate ranked lowest by the tabulation of votes, the next shortest term to the next-lowest ranked elected candidate, and so on. In the case of a tie among those eligible for a incomplete term, the [verifiable random selection procedure](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#random)
 described below will be used to assign the incomplete term.

Refer to [How to Organize an Advisory Board or TAG election](https://www.w3.org/2002/10/election-howto)
 [\[ELECTION-HOWTO\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-election-howto "How to Organize an Advisory Board or TAG election")
 for more details.

##### 3.3.3.4. Technical Architecture Group Appointments[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TAG-appointments)

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 is responsible for appointing 3 of the participants to the [Technical Architecture Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
. This mechanism complements the election process. The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _should_ use its appointments to support a diverse and well-balanced [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
, including diversity of technical background, knowledge, and skill sets.

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _should_ actively seek candidates for appointment to the TAG, and _must_ make available to the W3C community at large a means to propose candidates for consideration, explicitly soliciting input from at least current and incoming [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 members, the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
, and [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 [Chairs](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
.

The constraints for appointment to the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 are the same as for elected participants (see [§ 3.3.3.2 Elected Groups Participation Constraints](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-constraints)
 and [§ 3.3.3.3 Advisory Board and Technical Architecture Group Elections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-elections)
), with the additional constraint that a person _must not_ be appointed for more than two _consecutive_ terms. (Partial terms used to fill a vacated seat do not count towards this limit.)

Note: Individuals who have reached the limit of two consecutive appointed terms _may_ freely run for election if they wish to continue serving on the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
.

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
's choice of appointee(s) is subject to ratification by secret ballot by both the [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 and the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
, each requiring a two-thirds approval. In the case of regularly scheduled elections, the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 participants in this ratification are its members for the upcoming term.

For regularly scheduled elections, selection begins once the results of the elections are known, and the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _should_ announce the ratified appointment(s) no later than the start of the regularly scheduled term. When an appointed seat is vacated outside of a regularly scheduled election, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _should_ seek to appoint a replacement unless a regular Call for Nominations is scheduled within 2 months, and it _must_ announce the ratified appointment no later than the Call for Nominations of the next scheduled election.

##### 3.3.3.5. Verifiable Random Selection Procedure[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#random)

When it is necessary to use a verifiable random selection process (e.g., in an [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 or [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 election, to “draw straws” in case of a tie or to fill a incomplete term), W3C uses the random and verifiable procedure defined in [RFC 3797](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc3797.txt)
 [\[RFC3797\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-rfc3797 "Publicly Verifiable Nominations Committee (NomCom) Random Selection")
. The procedure orders an input list of names (listed in alphabetical order by family name unless otherwise specified) into a “result order”.

W3C applies this procedure as follows:

1.  When N people have tied for M (less than N) seats. In this case, only the names of the N individuals who tied are provided as input to the procedure. The M seats are assigned in result order.
2.  After all elected individuals have been identified, when N people are eligible for M (less than N) incomplete terms. In this case, only the names of those N individuals are provided as input to the procedure. The incomplete terms are assigned in result order.

##### 3.3.3.6. Elected Groups Vacated Seats[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-vacated)

An [Advisory Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 or [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 participant’s seat is vacated when:

*   the participant resigns, or
*   an Advisory Board or TAG participant changes affiliations such that the [Advisory Board and TAG participation constraints](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-constraints)
     are no longer met, or
*   the [CEO](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ceo)
     dismisses the participant for failing to meet the criteria in section [§ 3.1.1 Individual Participation Criteria](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ParticipationCriteria)
    , or
*   their term ends.

If a participant changes affiliation, but the [participation constraints](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-constraints)
 are met, that participant’s seat becomes vacant at the next regularly scheduled election for that group.

Vacated seats are filled according to this schedule:

*   When an appointed [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
     seat is vacated, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
     [appoints](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TAG-appointments)
     a replacement.
*   When an elected seat on either the [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
     or [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
     is vacated, the seat is filled at the next regularly scheduled election for the group unless the group Chair requests that W3C hold an election before then (for instance, due to the group’s workload).
    
    *   The group Chair _should not_ request such an election if the next regularly scheduled election is fewer than three months away.
    *   The group Chair _may_ request an election, and the election may begin, as soon as a current member gives notice of a resignation, including a resignation effective as of a given date in the future.
    
    When such an election is held, the minimum number of available seats is such that when added to the number of continuing participants, the minimum total number of elected seats is met (8 for the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
    , 9 for the [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
    ); and the maximum number corresponds to all unoccupied seats. Except for the number of available seats and the length of the terms, the [usual rules for Advisory Board and Technical Architecture Group Elections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-elections)
     apply.
    

### 3.4. Chartered Groups: Working Groups and Interest Groups[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GAGeneral)

This document defines two types of chartered groups:

[Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
.

Working Groups typically produce deliverables (e.g., [Recommendation Track technical reports](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#rec-advance)
, software, test suites, and reviews of the deliverables of other groups) as defined in their [charter](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#WGCharter)
.

Working Groups have additional participation requirements described in the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
, see particularly the “[Licensing Obligations of Working Group Participants](https://www.w3.org/policies/patent-policy/#sec-Obligations)
” and the patent claim exclusion process in “[Exclusion From W3C RF Licensing Requirements](https://www.w3.org/policies/patent-policy/#sec-Exclusion)
”.

[Interest Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsIG)
.

The primary goal of an Interest Group is to bring together people who wish to evaluate potential Web technologies and policies. An Interest Group is a forum for the exchange of ideas.

Interest Groups do not publish [Recommendation Track technical reports](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
; but can publish [technical reports on the Note Track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#WGNote)
.

#### 3.4.1. Requirements for All Chartered Groups[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ReqsAllGroups)

Each group _must_ have a [charter](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#charter)
. Requirements for the charter depend on the group type. All group charters _must_ be public (even if other proceedings of the group are [Member-only](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Member-only)
).

Each group _must_ have a Chair (or co-Chairs) to coordinate the group’s tasks. The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 appoints (and re-appoints) Chairs for all groups. The Chair is a [Member representative](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-rep)
, a [Team representative](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Team)
, or an [Invited Expert](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#invited-expert-wg)
, (invited by the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
). The requirements of this document that apply to those types of participants apply to Chairs as well.

Note: The [role of the Chair](https://www.w3.org/Guide/chair/role.html)
 [\[CHAIR\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-chair "W3C Working/Interest Group Chair")
 is described in the [Art of Consensus](https://www.w3.org/Guide/)
 [\[GUIDE\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-guide "The Art of Consensus, a guidebook for W3C Working Group Chairs and other collaborators")
.

Each group _must_ have a Team Contact, who acts as the interface between the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
, group participants, and the rest of the Team.

Note: The [role of the Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/Guide/teamcontact/role.html)
 [\[TEAM-CONTACT\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-team-contact "Role of the Team Contact")
 is described in the [Art of Consensus](https://www.w3.org/Guide/)
 [\[GUIDE\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-guide "The Art of Consensus, a guidebook for W3C Working Group Chairs and other collaborators")
.

The [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 and the [Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TeamContact)
 of a group _should not_ be the same individual.

Each group _must_ have an archived mailing list for formal group communication (e.g., for meeting announcements and [minutes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#minutes)
, documentation of decisions, and [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 to decisions). It is the responsibility of the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 and [Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TeamContact)
 to ensure that new participants are subscribed to all relevant mailing lists. Refer to the list of [group mailing lists](https://www.w3.org/groups/)
 [\[GROUP-MAIL\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-group-mail "Group mailing lists")
.

A [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 _may_ form task forces (composed of group participants) to carry out assignments for the group. The scope of these assignments _must not_ exceed the scope of the group’s charter. A group _should_ document the process it uses to create task forces (e.g., each task force might have an informal "charter"). Task forces do not publish [technical reports](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Reports)
; the Working Group _may_ choose to publish their results as part of a technical report.

#### 3.4.2. Participation in Chartered Groups[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#group-participation)

There are three types of individual participants in a Working Group: [Member representatives](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-rep)
, [Invited Experts](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#invited-expert-wg)
, and [Team representatives](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Team)
 (including the [Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TeamContact)
).

There are four types of individual participants in an Interest Group: the same three types as for Working Groups plus, for an Interest Group where the only [participation requirement is mailing list subscription](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ig-mail-only)
, public participants.

Except where noted in this document or in a group charter, all participants share the same rights and responsibilities in a group; see also the [individual participation criteria](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ParticipationCriteria)
.

A participant _may_ represent more than one organization in a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 or [Interest Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsIG)
. Those organizations _must_ all be members of the group.

An individual _may_ become a Working or Interest Group participant at any time during the group’s existence. See also relevant requirements in “Joining an Already Established Working Group” in the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
.

On an exceptional basis, a Working or Interest Group participant _may_ designate a substitute to attend a [meeting](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralMeetings)
 and _should_ inform the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
. The substitute _may_ act on behalf of the participant, including for [votes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Votes)
. For the substitute to vote, the participant _must_ inform the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 in writing in advance. As a courtesy to the group, if the substitute is not well-versed in the group’s discussions, the regular participant _should_ authorize another participant to act as [proxy](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#proxy)
 for votes.

To allow rapid progress, Working Groups are intended to be small (typically fewer than 15 people) and composed of experts in the area defined by the charter. In principle, Interest Groups have no limit on the number of participants. When a Working Group grows too large to be effective, W3C _may_ split it into an Interest Group (a discussion forum) and a much smaller Working Group (a core group of highly dedicated participants).

#### 3.4.3. Types of Participants in Chartered Groups[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#chartered-group-participants)

##### 3.4.3.1. Member Representative in a Working Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-rep-wg)

An individual is a Member representative in a Working Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-representative-in-a-working-group)
 if all of the following conditions are satisfied:

*   the Advisory Committee representative of the Member in question has designated the individual as a Working Group participant, and
*   the individual qualifies for [Member representation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-rep)
    .

To designate an individual as a Member representative in a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
, an [Advisory Committee representative](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
 _must_ provide the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 and [Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TeamContact)
 with all of the following information, in addition to any other information required by the [Call for Participation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#cfp)
 and charter (including the participation requirements of the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
):

1.  The name of the W3C Member the individual represents and whether the individual is an employee of that Member organization;
2.  A statement that the individual accepts the participation terms set forth in the charter (with an indication of charter date or version);
3.  A statement that the Member will provide the necessary financial support for participation (e.g., for travel, telephone calls, and conferences).

A Member participates in a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 from the moment the first Member representative joins the group until either of the following occurs:

*   the group closes, or
*   the Member [resigns](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#resignation)
     from the Working Group; this is done through the Member’s Advisory Committee representative.

##### 3.4.3.2. Member Representative in an Interest Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-rep-ig)

When the participation requirements exceed [Interest Group mailing list subscription](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ig-mail-only)
, an individual is a Member representative in an Interest Group if all of the following conditions are satisfied:

*   the [Advisory Committee representative](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
     of the Member in question has designated the individual as an Interest Group participant, and
*   the individual qualifies for [Member representation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-rep)
    .

To designate an individual as a [Member representative in an Interest Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-representative-in-an-interest-group)
, the Advisory Committee representative _must_ follow the instructions in the [Call for Participation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#cfp)
 and charter.

Member participation in an Interest Group ceases under the same conditions as for a Working Group.

##### 3.4.3.3. Invited Expert in a Working Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#invited-expert-wg)

The [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 _may_ invite an individual with a particular expertise to participate in a Working Group. This individual _may_ represent an organization in the group (e.g., if acting as a liaison with another organization).

An individual is an Invited Expert in a Working Group if all of the following conditions are satisfied:

*   the Chair has designated the individual as a group participant,
*   the Team Contact has agreed with the Chair’s choice, and
*   the individual has provided the [information required of an Invited Expert](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#inv-expert-info)
     to the Chair and Team Contact.

To designate an individual as an Invited Expert in a Working Group, the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 _must_ inform the Team Contact and provide rationale for the choice. When the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 and the [Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TeamContact)
 disagree about a designation, the [CEO](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ceo)
 determines whether the individual will be invited to participate in the Working Group.

To participate in a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 as an Invited Expert, an individual _must_:

*   identify the organization, if any, the individual represents as a participant in this group,
*   agree to the terms of the [invited expert and collaborators agreement](https://www.w3.org/Consortium/Legal/collaborators-agreement)
     [\[COLLABORATORS-AGREEMENT\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-collaborators-agreement "Invited expert and collaborators agreement")
    ,
*   accept the participation terms set forth in the charter, including the participation requirements of the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
    , especially in “Note on Licensing Commitments for Invited Experts” and in “Disclosure”, indicating a specific charter date or version,
*   disclose whether the individual is an employee of a W3C Member; see the [conflict of interest policy](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#coi)
    ,
*   provide a statement of who will provide the necessary financial support for the individual’s participation (e.g., for travel, telephone calls, and conferences), and
*   if the individual’s employer (including a self-employed individual) or the organization the individual represents is not a W3C Member, indicate whether that organization intends to join W3C. If the organization does not intend to join W3C, indicate reasons the individual is aware of for this choice.

The [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 _should not_ designate as an [Invited Expert in a Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#invited-expert-in-a-working-group)
 an individual who is an employee of a W3C Member. The Chair _must not_ use Invited Expert status to circumvent participation limits imposed by the [charter](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#WGCharter)
.

An Invited Expert participates in a Working Group from the moment the individual joins the group until any of the following occurs:

*   the group closes, or
*   the Chair or [CEO](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ceo)
     withdraws the invitation to participate, or
*   the individual [resigns](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#resignation)
    .

##### 3.4.3.4. Invited Expert in an Interest Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#invited-expert-ig)

When the participation requirements exceed [Interest Group mailing list subscription](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ig-mail-only)
, the participation requirements for an Invited Expert in an Interest Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#invited-expert-in-an-interest-group)
 are the same as those for an [Invited Expert in a Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#invited-expert-in-a-working-group)
.

##### 3.4.3.5. Team Representative in a Working Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team-rep-wg)

An individual is a Team representative in a Working Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team-representative-in-a-working-group)
 when so designated by W3C management. Team representatives both contribute to the technical work and help ensure the group’s proper integration with the rest of W3C.

A Team representative participates in a Working Group from the moment the individual joins the group until any of the following occurs:

*   the group closes, or
*   W3C management changes Team representation by sending email to the Chair, copying the group mailing list.

The Team participates in a Working Group from the moment the creation of the group is announced until the group closes.

##### 3.4.3.6. Team Representative in an Interest Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team-rep-ig)

When the participation requirements exceed [Interest Group mailing list subscription](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ig-mail-only)
, an individual is a Team representative in an Interest Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team-representative-in-an-interest-group)
 when so designated by W3C management.

4\. Lifecycle of Chartered Groups[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#group-lifecyle)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4.1. Initiating Charter Development[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#WGCharterDevelopment)

W3C creates charters for [chartered groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#chartered-group)
 based on interest from the Members and Team. The Team _must_ notify the Advisory Committee when a charter for a new Working Group or Interest Group is in development. This is intended to raise awareness, even if no formal proposal is yet available. Advisory Committee representatives _may_ provide feedback on the [Advisory Committee discussion list](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACCommunication)
 or via other designated channels.

W3C _may_ begin work on a Working Group or Interest Group charter at any time.

### 4.2. Content of a Charter[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#WGCharter)

A Working Group or Interest Group charter _must_ include all of the following information.

*   The group’s mission (e.g., develop a technology or process, review the work of other groups).
*   The scope of the group’s work and criteria for success.
*   The duration of the group (typically from six months to two years).
*   The nature of any deliverables (technical reports, reviews of the deliverables of other groups, or software).
*   Expected milestone dates where available.
    
    Note: A charter is _not required_ to include schedules for review of other group’s deliverables.
    
*   The process for the group to approve the release of deliverables (including intermediate results).
*   Any dependencies by groups within or outside of W3C on the deliverables of this group. For any dependencies, the charter _must_ specify the mechanisms for communication about the deliverables.
*   Any dependencies of this group on other groups within or outside of W3C. Such dependencies include interactions with [W3C Horizontal Groups](https://www.w3.org/Guide/process/charter.html#horizontal-review)
     [\[CHARTER\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-charter "How to Create a Working Group or Interest Group")
    .
*   The [level of confidentiality](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#confidentiality-levels)
     of the group’s proceedings and deliverables.
*   Meeting mechanisms and expected frequency.
*   If known, the date of the first [face-to-face meeting](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ftf-meeting)
    . The date of the first face-to-face meeting of a proposed group _must not_ be sooner than eight weeks after the date of the proposal.
*   Communication mechanisms to be employed within the group, between the group and the rest of W3C, and with the general public.
*   Any voting procedures or requirements other than those specified in [§ 5.2.3 Deciding by Vote](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Votes)
    .
*   An estimate of the expected time commitment from participants.
*   The expected time commitment and level of involvement by the Team (e.g., to track developments, write and edit technical reports, develop code, or organize pilot experiments).
*   Intellectual property information. What are the intellectual property (including patents and copyright) considerations affecting the success of the Group? In particular, is there any reason to believe that it will be difficult to meet the Royalty-Free licensing goals in “Licensing Goals for W3C Specifications” in the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
    ?

See also the charter requirements in “Licensing Goals for W3C Specifications” in the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
.

For every Recommendation Track deliverable that continues work on [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 published under any other Charter (including a predecessor group of the same name), for which there is at least an existing [First Public Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#fpwd)
 the description of that deliverable in the proposed charter of the adopting Working Group _must_ provide the following information:

*   The title, stable URL, and publication date of the [Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsWD)
     or other Recommendation-track document that will serve as the basis for work on the deliverable (labeled “Adopted Draft”);
*   The title, stable URL, and publication date of the document that was used as the basis for its most recent Exclusion Opportunity as per the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
    . (labeled “Exclusion Draft”); and
*   The stable URL of the Working Group charter under which the Exclusion Draft was published (labeled the “Exclusion Draft Charter[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#exclusion-draft-charter)
    ”).

All of the above data _must_ be identified in the adopting Working Group’s charter using the labels indicated.

The [Adopted Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#adopted-draft)
 and the [Exclusion Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#exclusion-draft)
 _must_ each be adopted in their entirety and without any modification. The proposed charter _must_ state the dates on which the Exclusion Opportunity that arose on publishing the [Exclusion Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#exclusion-draft)
 began and ended. As per “Joining an Already Established Working Group” in the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
, this potentially means that exclusions can only be made immediately on joining a Working Group.

An Interest Group charter _may_ include provisions regarding participation, including specifying that the only requirement for participation (by anyone) in the Interest Group is subscription to the Interest Group mailing list. This type of Interest Group _may_ have [public participants](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#public-participant-ig)
.

A charter _may_ include provisions other than those required by this document. The charter _should_ highlight whether additional provisions impose constraints beyond those of the W3C Process Document (e.g., limits on the number of individuals in a Working Group who represent the same Member organization or group of [related Members](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#MemberRelated)
).

### 4.3. Advisory Committee Review of a Charter[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#CharterReview)

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ solicit [Advisory Committee review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACReview)
 of every new or substantively modified Working Group or Interest Group charter, except for either:

*   a charter extension
    
*   substantive changes to a charter that do not affect the way the group functions in any significant way.
    

The review period _must_ be at least 28 days. The following are examples of substantive changes that would not require an [Advisory Committee Review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
: the addition of an in-scope deliverable, a change of [Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TeamContact)
, or a change of [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
. Such changes must nonetheless be announced to the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 and to [participants in the Working](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#wgparticipant)
 or [in the Interest Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#igparticipant)
, and a rationale must be provided.

The Call for Review of a substantively modified charter _must_ highlight important changes (e.g., regarding deliverables or resource allocation) and include rationale for the changes.

As part of the Advisory Committee review of any new or substantively modified Working Group charter, any Advisory Committee representative _may_ request an extended review period.

Such a request must be submitted with a Member’s comments in response to the Call for Review. Upon receipt of any such request, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ ensure that the Call for Participation for the Working Group occurs at least 60 days after the Call for Review of the charter.

### 4.4. Call for Participation in a Chartered Group[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#cfp)

Deciding whether to adopt a proposed [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 or [Interest Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsIG)
 [charter](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#charter)
 is a [W3C Decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-w3c-decision)
. Charters _may_ be amended based on review comments per [§ 5.7.2 After the Review Period](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACReviewAfter)
 before the Call for Participation.

If the decision is to charter the group, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ issue a Call for Participation to the Advisory Committee. For a new group, this announcement officially creates the group. The announcement _must_ include a reference to the [charter](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#WGCharter)
, the name(s) of the group’s [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
(s), and the name(s) of the [Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TeamContact)
(s).

After a Call for Participation, any [Member representatives](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-rep)
 and [Invited Experts](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#invited-expert-wg)
 _must_ be designated (or re-designated). When a group is re-chartered, individuals participating in the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 or [Interest Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsIG)
 before the new Call for Participation may attend any meetings held within forty-five (45) days of the Call for Participation even if they have not yet formally rejoined the group (i.e., committed to the terms of the charter and patent policy).

Advisory Committee representatives _may_ initiate an [Advisory Committee Appeal](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACAppeal)
 against the decision to create or substantially modify a Working Group or Interest Group charter.

### 4.5. Charter Extension[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#charter-extension)

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _may_ decide to extend a Working Group or Interest Group charter with no other substantive modifications. The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ announce such extensions to the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
. The announcement _must_ indicate the new duration. The announcement _must_ also include rationale for the extension, a reference to the [charter](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#WGCharter)
, and the Group homepage (which includes at least the name(s) of the group’s [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
(s), the name of the [Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TeamContact)
, and instructions for joining the group).

After a charter extension, Advisory Committee representatives and the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 are _not required_ to re-designate [Member representatives](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#member-rep)
 and [Invited Experts](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#invited-expert-wg)
.

Advisory Committee representatives _may_ initiate an [Advisory Committee Appeal](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACAppeal)
 against a [Team decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team-decision)
 regarding the extension of a Working Group or Interest Group charter.

### 4.6. Chartered Group Closure[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralTermination)

A [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 or [Interest Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsIG)
 charter specifies a duration for the group.

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
, the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
, or the [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 _may_ propose to close a group prior to the date specified in the charter in any of the following circumstances:

*   There are insufficient member resources to produce chartered deliverables or to maintain the group, according to priorities established within W3C.
    
*   A [Patent Advisory Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/patent-policy/#patent-advisory-group)
     concluded that the work _should_ be terminated.
    
*   The [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
     or [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
     determined that continuing operation of the chartered group or its work would be detrimental to W3C or its mission.
    
*   The group produced all chartered deliverables ahead of schedule.
    

Such a proposal to close a group must be accompanied by rationale, and the proposal must be confirmed by an [AC Review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
 as a [W3C Decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-w3c-decision)
.

Closing a Working Group has implications with respect to the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
.

5\. Decisions[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#decisions)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------

W3C attempts to resolve issues through dialog. Individuals who disagree strongly with a decision _should_ register with the Chair any [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
.

### 5.1. Types of Decisions[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#decision-types)

The [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 of a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 or [Interest Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsIG)
 has the prerogative to make certain decisions based on their own judgment. Such decisions are called chair decisions[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#chair-decisions)
.

In contrast, decisions taken by the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 of a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 or [Interest Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsIG)
 on the basis of having assessed the [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
 of the group or following a vote (see [§ 5.2.3 Deciding by Vote](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Votes)
) are called group decisions (also known as group “resolutions”).

Decisions made by members of the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 in connection with this Process, based on their own individual or collective judgement, are called Team Decisions.

In contrast, a W3C decision is determined by the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 on behalf of the W3C community by assessing the consensus of the W3C Community after an [Advisory Committee review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
.

### 5.2. Consensus Building[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#consensus-building)

#### 5.2.1. Consensus[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Consensus)

Consensus is a core value of W3C. To promote consensus, the W3C process requires Chairs to ensure that groups consider all legitimate views and objections, and endeavor to resolve them, whether these views and objections are expressed by the active participants of the group or by others (e.g., another W3C group, a group in another organization, or the general public). Decisions _may_ be made during meetings ([face-to-face](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ftf-meeting)
 or [distributed](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#distributed-meeting)
) as well as through [persistent text-based discussions](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#discussion-archiving)
.

Note: The CEO has the role of assessing consensus within the Advisory Committee.

The following terms are used in this document to describe the level of support for a decision among a set of eligible individuals:

Consensus:

A substantial number of individuals in the set support the decision and there is no sustained objection from anybody in the set. Individuals in the set _may_ abstain. Abstention is either an explicit expression of no opinion or silence by an individual in the set.

Unanimity:

The particular case of [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
 where all individuals in the set support the decision (i.e., no individual in the set abstains).

Dissent:

At least one individual in the set sustains an objection.

Note: A [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 always indicates a sustained objection, but isn’t necessary to express it (except in the context of formal [AC Reviews](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
). Disagreement with a proposed decision, however, does not always rise to the level of sustained objection, as individuals could be willing to accept a decision while expressing disagreement.

By default, the set of individuals eligible to participate in a decision is the set of group participants. The Process Document does not require a quorum for decisions (i.e., the minimal number of eligible participants required to be present before the Chair can call a question). A charter _may_ include a quorum requirement for consensus decisions.

Where [unanimity](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Unanimity)
 is not possible, a group _should_ strive to make [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
 decisions where there is significant support and few abstentions. The Process Document does not require a particular percentage of eligible participants to agree to a motion in order for a decision to be made. To avoid decisions where there is widespread apathy, (i.e., little support and many abstentions), groups _should_ set minimum thresholds of active support before a decision can be recorded. The appropriate percentage _may_ vary depending on the size of the group and the nature of the decision. A charter _may_ include threshold requirements for consensus decisions. For instance, a charter might require a supermajority of eligible participants (i.e., some established percentage above 50%) to support certain types of consensus decisions.

Note: Chairs have substantial flexibility in how they obtain and assess consensus among their groups. Unless otherwise constrained by charter, they may use modes including but not limited to explicit calls for consensus, polls of participants, “lazy consensus” in which lack of objection after sufficient notice is taken as assent; or they may also delegate and empower a document editor to assess consensus on their behalf, whether in general or for specific pre-determined circumstances (e.g. in non-controversial situations, for specific types of issues, etc.).

If questions or disagreements arise, the final determination of consensus remains with the chair.

#### 5.2.2. Managing Dissent[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#managing-dissent)

In some cases, even after careful consideration of all points of view, a group might find itself unable to reach consensus. The [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 _may_ record a decision where there is [dissent](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Dissent)
 so that the group can make progress (for example, to produce a deliverable in a timely manner). Dissenters cannot stop a group’s work simply by saying that they cannot live with a decision. When the Chair believes that the Group has duly considered the legitimate concerns of dissenters as far as is possible and reasonable, the group _should_ move on.

Groups _should_ favor proposals that create the weakest objections. This is preferred over proposals that are supported by a large majority but that cause strong objections from a few people. As part of making a decision where there is [dissent](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Dissent)
, the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 is expected to be aware of which participants work for the same (or [related](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#MemberRelated)
) Member organizations and weigh their input accordingly.

Note: Dissenters can escalate their sustained objection to a decision by registering a [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
.

#### 5.2.3. Deciding by Vote[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Votes)

A group _should_ only conduct a vote to resolve a _substantive issue_ after the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 has determined that all available means of [reaching consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Consensus)
 through technical discussion and compromise have failed, and that a vote is necessary to break a deadlock. In this case the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 _must_ record (e.g., in the [minutes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#minutes)
 of the meeting or in an archived email message):

*   an explanation of the issue being voted on;
*   the decision to conduct a vote (e.g., a simple majority vote) to resolve the issue;
*   the outcome of the vote;
*   any Formal Objections.

In order to vote to resolve a substantive issue, an individual _must_ be a group [participant](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#participant)
. Each organization represented in the group _must_ have at most one vote, even when the organization is represented by several participants in the group (including Invited Experts). For the purposes of voting:

*   A Member or group of [related Members](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#MemberRelated)
     is considered a single organization.
*   The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Team)
     is considered an organization.

Unless the charter states otherwise, [Invited Experts](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#invited-expert-wg)
 _may_ vote.

If a participant is unable to attend a vote, that individual _may_ authorize anyone at the meeting to act as a proxy. The absent participant _must_ inform the Chair in writing who is acting as proxy, with written instructions on the use of the proxy. For a Working Group or Interest Group, see the related requirements regarding an individual who attends a meeting as a [substitute](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#mtg-substitute)
 for a participant.

A group _may_ vote for other purposes than to resolve a substantive issue. For instance, the Chair often conducts a “straw poll” vote as a means of determining whether there is consensus about a potential decision.

A group _may_ also vote to make a process decision. For example, it is appropriate to decide by simple majority whether to hold a meeting in San Francisco or San Jose (there’s not much difference geographically). When simple majority votes are used to decide minor issues, voters are _not required_ to state the reasons for votes, and the group is _not required_ to record individual votes.

A group charter _may_ include formal voting procedures (e.g., quorum or threshold requirements) for making decisions about substantive issues.

### 5.3. Formally Addressing an Issue[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#formal-address)

In the context of this document, a group has formally addressed an issue when it has sent a public, substantive response to the reviewer who raised the issue. A substantive response is expected to include rationale for decisions (e.g., a technical explanation, a pointer to charter scope, or a pointer to a requirements document). The adequacy of a response is measured against what a W3C reviewer would generally consider to be technically sound. If a group believes that a reviewer’s comments result from a misunderstanding, the group _should_ seek clarification before reaching a decision.

As a courtesy, both Chairs and reviewers _should_ set expectations for the schedule of responses and acknowledgments. The group _should_ reply to a reviewer’s initial comments in a timely manner. The group _should_ set a time limit for acknowledgment by a reviewer of the group’s substantive response; a reviewer cannot block a group’s progress. It is common for a reviewer to require a week or more to acknowledge and comment on a substantive response. The group’s responsibility to respond to reviewers does not end once a reasonable amount of time has elapsed. However, reviewers _should_ realize that their comments will carry less weight if not sent to the group in a timely manner.

Substantive responses _should_ be recorded. The group _should_ maintain an accurate summary of all substantive issues and responses to them (e.g., in the form of an issues list with links to mailing list archives).

### 5.4. Reopening a Decision When Presented With New Information[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#WGChairReopen)

The [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 _may_ reopen a decision when presented with new information, including:

*   additional technical information,
*   comments by email from participants who were unable to attend a scheduled meeting,
*   comments by email from meeting attendees who chose not to speak out during a meeting (e.g., so they could confer later with colleagues or for cultural reasons).

The [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 _should_ record that a decision has been reopened, and _must_ do so upon request from a group participant.

### 5.5. Registering Formal Objections[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#registering-objections)

Any individual (regardless of whether they are associated with a Member) _may_ appeal any decision made in connection with this Process (except those having a different appeal process) by registering a Formal Objection with the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
. Group participants _should_ inform their [Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#TeamContact)
 as well as the group’s Chair(s). The Team Contact _must_ inform the [CEO](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ceo)
 when a group participant has also raised concerns about due process.

Note: In this document, the term [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 is used to emphasize this process implication: Formal Objections receive formal consideration and a formal response. The word “objection” used alone has its ordinary English connotations. See [§ 5.2 Consensus Building](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#consensus-building)
.

A [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 _must_ include a summary of the issue (whether technical or procedural), the decision being appealed, and the rationale for the objection. It _should_ cite technical arguments and propose changes that would remove the [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
; these proposals _may_ be vague or incomplete. [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 that do not provide substantive arguments or rationale are unlikely to receive serious consideration. Counter-arguments, rationales, and decisions _should_ also be recorded.

A record of each [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 against a decision regarding a publicly-available document _must_ be made [publicly available](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#confidentiality-change)
; likewise, a record of each [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 against a [Member-visible](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Member-only)
 decision _must_ be made [available to Members](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#confidentiality-change)
. A Call for Review to the Advisory Committee _must_ identify any [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 related to that review.

Note: [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 against matter in a [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 are required to be addressed before [requesting advancement](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#rec-advance)
 of the [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
.

A [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 filed during an [Advisory Committee Review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
 is considered registered at the close of the review period.

### 5.6. Addressing Formal Objections[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#addressing-fo)

#### 5.6.1. Investigation and Mediation by the Team[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team-fo-mediation)

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 considers the [Formal objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
, researches the question, interviews parties, and so on, to make sure the problem and the various viewpoints are well understood, and to the extent possible, to arrive at a recommended disposition. If the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 can resolve the issue to the satisfaction of the individual that filed the [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
, the individual withdraws the objection and the disposition process terminates.

Otherwise, upon concluding that consensus cannot be found, and no later than 90 days after the [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 being registered, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ initiate formation of a [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
, which should be [convened](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-convention)
 within 45 days of being initiated. Concurrently, it must prepare a report for the [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 documenting its findings and attempts to find consensus.

#### 5.6.2. W3C Council[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council)

A W3C Council is the body convened to resolve [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 by combining the capabilities and perspectives of the [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
, the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
, and the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
, and is tasked with doing so in the best interests of the Web and W3C.

##### 5.6.2.1. Council Composition[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-composition)

Each [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 is composed of the following members (excepting any [renounced or dismissed](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-participation)
):

*   the [CEO](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ceo)
     or their chosen [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
     delegate
    
*   the members of the [Advisory Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
    
*   the members of the [Technical Architecture Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
    

Participation in a [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 _must not_ require attendance of [face-to-face meetings](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ftf-meeting)
.

A distinct instance of the [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 is convened for each decision being appealed or objected to. Membership of each [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 instance is fixed at formation, and is not changed by any [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 or [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 elections occurring before that Council has reached a conclusion. However, if participation in a [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 falls so low as to hinder effective and balanced deliberations, the [W3C Council Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council-chair)
 _should_ dissolve the [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 and call for a new one to be convened.

A [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 member is assigned to act as the Council Team Contact, to support this Council and to facilitate adherence to this Process.

##### 5.6.2.2. Extraordinary Delegation[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-delegation)

In extraordinary cases, if they feel a [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 would not be the appropriate deciding body, a member of the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 (particularly the Legal Counsel) or any potential Council member may suggest that the decision for that specific Formal Objection be delegated to the W3C Board of Directors, to an officer of its corporation (such as the Legal Counsel), or to one or more specific individuals from the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
. The potential Council members then _may_ confidentially discuss and _must_ vote whether to delegate the decision for that specific [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
. A decision to delegate _must_ be supported by a two-thirds supermajority vote (i.e., at least twice as many votes in favor as against). Delegation in such cases cannot be later revoked.

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ inform the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 when a [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 has been delegated, and to whom it has been delegated.

##### 5.6.2.3. Council Participation, Dismissal, and Renunciation[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-participation)

A potential Council member may be dismissed from the Council.
