In order to apply consistent criteria, the potential Council members decide collectively which reasons against service rise to a sufficient level for a potential member to be [dismissed](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dismissed)
. No-one is automatically dismissed, and individual recusal is not used in the Council. [Dismissal](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dismissed)
 applies to an individual person in the context of a specific Council, and should be used rarely in order to preserve the greatest diversity on the Council.

Note: A W3C Council is a deliberative body whose purpose is to find the best way forward for the Web and for W3C. It is not a judicial body tasked with determining right or wrong.

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ draft a list of potential [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 members, with annotations of possible reasons for dismissal against each one. The W3C community, including members and team, and potential council members, _must_ be given an opportunity to contribute possible reasons to this list. Affected members _must_ be given an opportunity to respond to such comments about themselves. The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 may report comments verbatim or may paraphrase them while preserving their intent; they may also elide inappropriate comments, such as any that violate applicable laws or the [\[CEPC\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-cepc "W3C Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct")
.

Before a Council forms, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 presents the entire list of potential members and collected reasons and responses to the potential Council members, who then consider for each potential member whether that individual’s participation would compromise the integrity of the Council decision, and vote whether to dismiss that potential member. No one is allowed to vote on their own dismissal; each dismissal is decided by simple majority of those not abstaining.

Note: Since dismissal is individual, when the decision being objected to was made by the [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 or [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 acting as a body, the entire [TAG](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-architecture-group)
 or [AB](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-board)
 is not expected or required to be dismissed.

An individual _may_ also renounce their seat on a Council, for strong reason, such as being forbidden by their employer to serve. The individual chooses the extent to which they explain their renunciation. Renunciation is disqualification from participation, not abstention, and _should not_ be used to excuse an absence of participation.

Any person who has been [dismissed](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dismissed)
 or who [renounces](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#renounce)
 their seat does not receive [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 materials, take part in its deliberations, help in the determination of consensus, or vote. The [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 _may_ still solicit and hear their testimony, as they can of anyone else in the W3C community.

##### 5.6.2.4. Unanimous Short Circuit[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-short-circuit)

The full Council process _may_ be short-circuited if the Team recommends a resolution and every potential member of a Council who is not renouncing their seat votes affirmatively (no abstentions) to adopt this resolution.

This step _may_ be run concurrently with [§ 5.6.2.3 Council Participation, Dismissal, and Renunciation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-participation)
 and prior to choosing a [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council-chair)
.

Note: This is intended for exceptional cases that don’t seem to warrant a full Council response because they are, for instance, too trivial, duplicative, etc.

##### 5.6.2.5. Council Chairing[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-chairing)

The Chair of each W3C Council is chosen by its members, by consensus if possible, falling back to a vote if that fails. The chair must be a member of that [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
. Chair selection happens during formation of each Council, and _must_ be re-run if requested by the [Council Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-team-contact)
 or by the [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 during the Council’s operation.

##### 5.6.2.6. Council Deliberations[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-deliberations)

Upon appointment of the [W3C Council Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council-chair)
 and delivery of the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
’s report, the [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 is considered to be convened and can start deliberations.

Having reviewed the information gathered by the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
, the Council _may_ conduct additional research or analysis, or request additional information or interviews from anyone, including the Team.

The Council _may_ further attempt to broker consensus, which, if successful, disposes the formal objection.

Otherwise, after sufficient deliberation, the [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 decides whether to uphold or overrule the objection. The [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 _may_ [overrule](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#overrule)
 the [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 even if it agrees with some of the supportive arguments.

When [upholding](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#uphold)
 an objection, it _should_ recommend a way forward. If the overturned decision has already had consequences (e.g., if the objection concerns material already in a published document) the Council _should_ suggest how these consequences might be mitigated. The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 is responsible for making sure that adequate mitigations are enacted in a timely fashion; and the [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 is not considered fully addressed until then.

Note: This does not create new powers for the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
, such as the ability to “unpublish” documents. The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
's role is to ensure the responsible parties enact adequate mitigations, by whatever means they already have at their disposal.

A Council _may_ form sub-groups for deliberation, who may return with a recommendation, but the full Council issues the final decision. The decision of the [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 _should_ be unanimous, and _may_ be issued under consensus. However, if despite careful deliberation a [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 is unable to reach consensus, the [W3C Council Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council-chair)
 may instead resort to voting. In that case, the decision is made by simple majority, with the [W3C Council Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council-chair)
 breaking any tie. In case of a vote, if two members of a Council who share the same affiliation cast an identical ballot, then their ballots count as a one vote, not two.

In the case of non-unanimous decisions, members of a [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 who disagree with the decision _may_ write a Minority Opinion explaining the reason for their disagreement.

The deliberations of a [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 are confidential to that [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 and its [Council Team Contact](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-team-contact)
.

If a [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 is unable to come to a conclusion within 45 days of being [convened](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-convention)
, the [W3C Council Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council-chair)
 _must_ inform the [AC](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 of this delay and of the status of the discussions. The [W3C Council Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council-chair)
 _may_ additionally make this report [public](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#confidentiality-levels)
.

##### 5.6.2.7. Council Decision Report[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-decision)

A [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 terminates by issuing a Council Report, which:

*   _must_ state whether the Council [upholds](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#uphold)
     or [overrules](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#overrule)
     the objection(s).
    
*   _must_ provide a rationale supporting the decision, which _should_ address each argument raised in the Formal Objection(s).
    
*   _must_ include any recommendation decided by the [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
    .
    
*   if the [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
     has been upheld, _should_ include any suggested [mitigations](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#fo-mitigations)
    .
    
*   _must_ include the [Minority Opinion](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#minority-opinion)
    (s), if any.
    
*   _must_ report the names of those who were [dismissed](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dismissed)
     or [renounced](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#renounce)
     their seat as well as those who were qualified to serve.
    
*   _must_ report the names of the individuals who participated in the final decision.
    
*   _may_ report vote totals, if any vote was held.
    
*   _must not_ attribute any position to any individual on the Council.
    

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ maintain a [public page on the W3C website](https://www.w3.org/about/council/)
 indexing all completed [Council Reports](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-report)
. If a Council decision is later overturned by an [AC Appeal](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-appeal)
, this _must_ also be mentioned. [Council Reports](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-report)
 _must_ be no more confidential than the decision or document being objected to.

The [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
 _may_ also issue a Supplemental Confidential Council Report[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#supplemental-confidential-council-report)
 with a more restricted [level of confidentiality](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#confidentiality-levels)
 than its main report when it believes that additional commentary on confidential aspects of the case would be informative. However, the main [Council Report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-report)
 _should_ be self-sufficient and understandable without reference to Supplemental Confidential Council Reports.

##### 5.6.2.8. Appealing Council Decisions[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-appeals)

[Advisory Committee representatives](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
 _may_ initiate an [Advisory Committee Appeal](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-appeal)
 of a Council decision issued in a [Council Report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#council-report)
.

### 5.7. Advisory Committee Reviews[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACReview)

Advisory Committee review is the process by which the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 formally confers its approval on [charters](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#charter)
, [technical reports](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
, and other matters.

#### 5.7.1. Start of a Review Period[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACReviewStart)

Each [Advisory Committee review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
 period begins with a Call for Review from the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 to the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
. The Call for Review describes the proposal, raises attention to deadlines, estimates when the decision will be available, and includes other practical information. Each Member organization _may_ send one review, which _must_ be returned by its [Advisory Committee representative](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
.

For clarity, in the context of an [AC Review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
, [dissent](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Dissent)
 _must_ be expressed as a [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
.

The Team _must_ provide two channels for Advisory Committee review comments:

1.  an archived [Team-only](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Team-only)
     channel;
2.  an archived [Member-only](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Member-only)
     channel.

The [Call for Review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#reviewform)
 _must_ specify which channel is the default for review comments on that Call.

Reviewers _may_ send information to either or both channels. A reviewer _may_ also share their own reviews with other Members on the [Advisory Committee discussion list](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACCommunication)
, and _may_ also make it available to the public.

A Member organization _may_ modify its review during a review period (e.g., in light of comments from other Members).

#### 5.7.2. After the Review Period[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACReviewAfter)

After the review period, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 determines the appropriate [W3C Decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-w3c-decision)
, which they _must_ announce to the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
. The announcement _must_ indicate the level of support for the proposal ([consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
 or [dissent](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Dissent)
), and specifically whether there were any [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
, with attention to [changing the confidentiality level](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#confidentiality-change)
 of the [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
.

If there were [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
, at least some of which were [upheld](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#uphold)
, or if there is not [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
 because of insufficient support, [W3C Decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-w3c-decision)
 _must_ be one of:

*   The proposal is returned for additional work, with a request to the initiator to improve the proposal.
*   The proposal is rejected.

If the proposal has [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
, or if any [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
 are retracted or overruled and the proposal otherwise has sufficient support to achieve [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
, this [W3C Decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-w3c-decision)
 _must_ be one of:

*   The proposal is adopted, possibly with additional changes integrated in order to address the comments of the [AC](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
     (see below).
*   The proposal is returned for additional work, with a request to the initiator to make desirable changes identified during the review and to resubmit.

If the proposal is adopted with changes other than [class 1 (markup) changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#class-1)
, then those changes _must_ be announced to the [AC](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 and to the [Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-group)
 that owns the document (if any).

Additionally, when adopting a proposal with [substantive changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#substantive-change)
 integrated, the announcement _must_ include rationale for the substantive changes, and those changes must have the [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
 of the subset of the [AC](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 that voted on the proposal (including anyone who explicitly abstained). For publications which have conditions in addition to [AC](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 approval for introducing [substantive changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#substantive-change)
 (such as [Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-group)
 consensus or implementation experience), those other conditions must also be re-fulfilled.

[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#example-978a60c7)
For example, to make [substantive changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#substantive-change)
 to a [Proposed Recommendations](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsPR)
, the [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 could be returned to [Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
. Alternatively, the desired changes can be introduced as non-substantive amendments using the process for [revising a Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#revising-rec)
. However, they cannot be directly integrated between [PR](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsPR)
 and [REC](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
, because that would fail to trigger a patent exclusion opportunity.

This document does not specify time intervals between the end of an [Advisory Committee review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
 period and the [W3C decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-w3c-decision)
. This is to ensure that the Members and [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 have sufficient time to consider comments gathered during the review. The [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 _should not_ expect an announcement sooner than two weeks after the end of a review period. If, after three weeks, the outcome has not been announced, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _should_ provide the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 with an update.

### 5.8. Advisory Committee Votes[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACVotes)

The [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 votes in [elections for seats on the TAG or Advisory Board](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#AB-TAG-elections)
, and in the event of an [Advisory Committee Appeal](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-appeal)
 achieving the required support to trigger an appeal vote. Whenever the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 votes, each Member or group of [related Members](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#related-member)
 has one vote.

### 5.9. Appeal by Advisory Committee Representatives[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#ACAppeal)

[Advisory Committee representatives](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
 _may_ appeal certain decisions, though appeals are only expected to occur in extraordinary circumstances.

When a [W3C decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-w3c-decision)
 is made following an [Advisory Committee review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
, [Advisory Committee representatives](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
 _may_ initiate an Advisory Committee Appeal. These [W3C decisions](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-w3c-decision)
 include those related to group creation and modification, and transitions to new maturity stages for Recommendation Track documents and the Process document.

[Advisory Committee representatives](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
 _may_ also initiate an appeal for decisions of a [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
, and for certain decisions that do not involve an [Advisory Committee review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
. These cases are identified in the sections which describe the requirements for the decision and include additional (non-reviewed) maturity stages of Recommendation Track documents, group [charter extensions](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dfn-charter-extension)
 and closures, and [Memoranda of Understanding](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#mou)
.

In all cases, an [appeal](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-appeal)
 _must_ be initiated within three weeks of the decision.

An [Advisory Committee representative](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
 initiates an [appeal](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-appeal)
 by sending a request to the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
, and should also share this request with the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
. The request should say “I appeal this Decision” and identify the decision, and may also include their rationale for appealing the decision.

Note: See [Appealing a W3C Decision](https://www.w3.org/Guide/process/ac-appeal)
 for a recommendation on how to communicate an appeal request to the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 and the [AC](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
.

Within one week the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ announce the appeal process to the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 and provide a mechanism for [Advisory Committee representatives](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-representative)
 to respond with a statement of positive support for this appeal. The archive of these statements _must_ be [member-only](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Member-only)
. If, within one week of the Team’s announcement, 5% or more of the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 support the appeal request, the Team _must_ organize an appeal vote asking the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 “Do you approve of the Decision?” together with links to the decision and the appeal support.

The ballot _must_ allow for three possible responses: “Approve”, “Reject”, and “Abstain”, together with Comments.

If the number of votes to reject exceeds the number of votes to approve, the decision is overturned. In that case, there are the following possible next steps:

1.  The proposal is rejected.
2.  The proposal is returned for additional work, after which the applicable decision process is re-initiated.

6\. W3C Technical Reports[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#Reports)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The W3C technical report development process is the set of steps and requirements followed by W3C [Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 to standardize Web technology. The W3C technical report development process is designed to:

*   support multiple specification development methodologies
*   maximize [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
     about the content of stable technical reports
*   ensure high technical and editorial quality
*   promote consistency among specifications
*   facilitate royalty-free, interoperable implementations of Web Standards, and
*   earn endorsement by W3C and the broader community.

See also “licensing goals for W3C Specifications” in the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
.

### 6.1. Types of Technical Reports[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#recs-and-notes)

This chapter describes the formal requirements for [publishing](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#publishing)
 and maintaining a [W3C Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
, [Note](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#WGNote)
, or [Registry Report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#registry-report)
.

Recommendations

[Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 develop technical reports on the [W3C Recommendation Track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-recommendation-track)
 in order to produce normative specifications or guidelines as standards for the Web. The [Recommendation Track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-recommendation-track)
 process incorporates requirements for [wide review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dfn-wide-review)
, [adequate implementation experience](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#adequate-implementation)
, and [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
\-building, and is subject to the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
, under which participants commit to Royalty-Free IPR licenses for implementations. See [§ 6.3 The W3C Recommendation Track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#rec-track)
 for details.

Notes

Groups can also publish documents as [W3C Notes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#WGNote)
 and [W3C Statements](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#statement)
, typically either to document information other than technical specifications, such as use cases motivating a specification and best practices for its use. See [§ 6.4 The Note Track (Notes and Statements)](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#note-track)
 for details.

Registries

[Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 can also publish [registries](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#registry)
 in order to document collections of values or other data. These are typically published in a separate [registry report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#registry-report)
, although they can also be directly embedded in [Recommendation Track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-recommendation-track)
 documents as a [registry section](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#registry-section)
. [Defining a registry](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#registry-definition)
 requires [wide review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dfn-wide-review)
 and [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
, but once set up, changes to registry entries are lightweight and can even be done without a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
. See [§ 6.5 The Registry Track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#registries)
 for details.

Individual [Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 and [Interest Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsIG)
 _should_ adopt additional processes for developing publications, so long as they do not conflict with the requirements in this chapter.

### 6.2. General Requirements for Technical Reports[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#general-requirements)

#### 6.2.1. Publication of Technical Reports[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#publication)

Publishing as used in this document refers to producing a version which is listed as a W3C Technical Report on its [Technical Reports index at https://www.w3.org/TR](https://www.w3.org/TR/)
 [\[TR\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-tr "The W3C technical reports index")
. Every document published as part of the technical report development process _must_ be a public document. W3C strives to make archival documents indefinitely available at their original address in their original form.

Every document published as part of the technical report development process _must_ clearly indicate its [maturity stage](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#maturity-stages)
, and _must_ include information about the status of the document. This status information:

*   _must_ be unique each time a specification is [published](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#publishing)
    ,
*   _must_ state which [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
     developed the specification,
*   _must_ state how to send comments or file bugs, and where these are recorded,
*   _must_ include expectations about next steps,
*   _should_ explain how the technology relates to existing international standards and related work inside or outside W3C, and
*   _should_ explain or link to an explanation of significant changes from the previous version.

Every Technical Report published as part of the Technical Report development process is edited by one or more editors appointed by a Group [Chair](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
. It is the responsibility of these editors to ensure that the decisions of the Group are correctly reflected in subsequent drafts of the technical report. An editor _must_ be a participant, per [§ 3.4.2 Participation in Chartered Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#group-participation)
 in the Group responsible for the document(s) they are editing.

The Team is _not required_ to publish a [Technical Report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 that does not conform to the Team’s [Publication Rules](https://www.w3.org/pubrules/)
 [\[PUBRULES\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-pubrules "Publication Rules")
 (e.g., for naming, status information, style, and copyright requirements). These rules are subject to change by the Team from time to time. The Team _must_ inform group [Chairs](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GeneralChairs)
 and the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
 of any changes to these rules.

The primary language for W3C [Technical Reports](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 is English. W3C encourages the translation of its [Technical Reports](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
. [Information about translations of W3C technical reports](https://www.w3.org/Consortium/Translation/)
 [\[TRANSLATION\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-translation "Translations of W3C technical reports")
 is available at the W3C website.

#### 6.2.2. Reviews and Review Responsibilities[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#doc-reviews)

A document is available for review from the moment it is first [published](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#publishing)
. Working Groups _should_ [formally address](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#formally-addressed)
 _any_ substantive review comment about a technical report in a timely manner.

Reviewers _should_ send substantive technical reviews as early as possible. [Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 are often reluctant to make [substantive changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#substantive-change)
 to a mature document, particularly if this would cause significant compatibility problems due to existing implementation. [Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 _should_ record substantive or interesting proposals raised by reviews but not incorporated into a current specification.

##### 6.2.2.1. Wide Review[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#wide-review)

The requirements for wide review are not precisely defined by the W3C Process. The objective is to ensure that the entire set of stakeholders of the Web community, including the general public, have had adequate notice of the progress of the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 (for example through notices posted to [public-review-announce@w3.org](https://lists.w3.org/Archives/Public/public-review-announce/)
) and were able to actually perform reviews of and provide comments on the specification. A second objective is to encourage groups to request reviews early enough that comments and suggested changes can still be reasonably incorporated in response to the review. Before approving transitions, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 will consider who has been explicitly offered a reasonable opportunity to review the document, who has provided comments, the record of requests to and responses from reviewers, especially [W3C Horizontal Groups](https://www.w3.org/Guide/process/charter.html#horizontal-review)
 [\[CHARTER\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-charter "How to Create a Working Group or Interest Group")
 and groups identified as dependencies in the charter or identified as [liaisons](https://www.w3.org/2001/11/StdLiaison.html)
 [\[LIAISON\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-liaison "W3C liaisons with other organizations")
, and seek evidence of clear communication to the general public about appropriate times and which content to review and whether such reviews actually occurred.

For example, inviting review of new or significantly revised sections published in Working Drafts, and tracking those comments and the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
's responses, is generally a good practice which would often be considered positive evidence of wide review. [Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 _should_ follow the [W3C Horizontal Groups](https://www.w3.org/Guide/documentreview/#how_to_get_horizontal_review)
’ review processes, and _should_ announce to other W3C Working Groups as well as the general public, especially those affected by this specification, a proposal to enter [Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 (for example in approximately 28 days). By contrast a generic statement in a document requesting review at any time is likely not to be considered as sufficient evidence that the group has solicited wide review.

A [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 could present evidence that wide review has been received, irrespective of solicitation. But it is important to note that receiving many detailed reviews is not necessarily the same as wide review, since they might only represent comment from a small segment of the relevant stakeholder community.

#### 6.2.3. Classes of Changes[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#correction-classes)

This document distinguishes the following 5 classes of changes to a document. The first two classes of change are considered editorial changes, the next two substantive changes, and the last one registry changes.

[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#class-1)

1.  No changes to text content
    

These changes include fixing broken links, style sheets, or invalid markup.

[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#class-2)

2.  Changes that do not functionally affect interpretation of the document
    

For [Recommendation-track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-recommendation-track)
 [technical reports](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 specifically, this constitutes changes that do not affect conformance, i.e. changes that reasonable implementers would not interpret as changing architectural or interoperability requirements or their implementation. Changes which resolve ambiguities in the specification are considered to change (by clarification) the implementation requirements and do not fall into this class.

Examples of changes in this class include correcting non-normative examples which clearly conflict with normative requirements, clarifying informative use cases or other non-normative text, fixing typos or grammatical errors where the change does not change requirements.

If there is any doubt or disagreement as to whether a change functionally affects interpretation, that change does not fall into this class.

[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#class-3)

3.  Other changes that do not add new features
    

For [Recommendation-track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-recommendation-track)
 documents, these changes _may_ affect conformance to the specification. A change that affects conformance is one that:

*   makes conforming data, processors, or other conforming agents become non-conforming according to the new version, or
*   makes non-conforming data, processors, or other agents become conforming, or
*   clears up an ambiguity or under-specified part of the specification in such a way that data, a processor, or an agent whose conformance was once unclear becomes clearly either conforming or non-conforming.

[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#class-4)

4.  New features
    

Changes that add new functionality, such as new elements, new APIs, new rules, etc.

[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#class-5)

5.  Changes to the contents of a [registry table](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#registry-table)
    

Changes that add, remove, or alter [registry entries](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#registry-entry)
 in a [registry table](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#registry-table)
.

#### 6.2.4. Errata Management[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#errata)

Tracking errors is an important part of a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
's ongoing care of a [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
; for this reason, the scope of a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 charter generally allows time for work after publication of a [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
. In this Process Document, the term “erratum” (plural “errata”) refers to any error that can be resolved by one or more changes in classes 1-3 of section [§ 6.2.3 Classes of Changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#correction-classes)
.

[Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 _must_ keep a public record of errors that are reported by readers and implementers for [Recommendations](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
. Such error reports _should_ be compiled no less frequently than quarterly.

[Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 decide how to document errata. Such documentation _must_ identify the affected [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 text and describe the error; it _may_ also describe some possible solution(s). Readers of the [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 _should_ be able easily to find and see the errata that apply to that specific [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 with their associated tests. Errata _may_ be documented in a separate errata page or tracking system. They _may_, in addition or alternatively, be annotated inline alongside the affected [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 text or at the start or end of the most relevant section(s).

#### 6.2.5. Candidate Amendments[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-amendments)

An [erratum](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#erratum)
 _may_ be accompanied by an informative, candidate correction approved by [group decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#group-decision)
. When annotated inline, errata—including their [candidate corrections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-correction)
—must be marked as such, are treated as [class 2 changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#correction-classes)
, and are published accordingly.

Note: Annotating changes in this way allows more mature documents such as [Recommendations](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 and [Candidate Recommendations](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 to be updated quickly with the Working Group’s most current thinking, even when the [candidate amendments](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-amendment)
 have not yet received sufficient review or implementation experience to be normatively incorporated into the specification proper.

A candidate addition is similar to a [candidate correction](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-correction)
, except that it proposes a new feature rather than an error correction.

If there is no group chartered to maintain a [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _may_ maintain its [errata](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#erratum)
 and associated [candidate corrections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-correction)
. Such corrections _must_ be marked as Team correction, and do not constitute a normative portion of the Recommendation, as defined in the Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
 (i.e. they are not covered by the Patent Policy). The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ solicit [wide review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dfn-wide-review)
 on [Team corrections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team-correction)
 that it produces.

[Candidate corrections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-correction)
 and [candidate additions](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-addition)
 are collectively known as candidate amendments.

In addition to their actual [maturity stage](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#maturity-stages)
, [published](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#publishing)
 [REC Track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-recommendation-track)
 documents with [candidate amendments](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-amendment)
 are also considered, for the purpose of the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
, to be [Working Drafts](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsWD)
 with those [candidate amendments](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-amendment)
 treated as normative.

#### 6.2.6. License Grants from Non-Participants[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#contributor-license)

When a party who is not already obligated under the Patent Policy offers a change in class 3 or 4 (as described in [§ 6.2.3 Classes of Changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#correction-classes)
) to a technical report under this process the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ request a recorded royalty-free patent commitment; for a change in class 4, the Team _must_ secure such commitment. Such commitment _should_ cover, at a minimum, all the party’s Essential Claims both in the contribution, and that become Essential Claims as a result of incorporating the contribution into the draft that existed at the time of the contribution, on the terms specified in the “W3C Royalty-Free (RF) Licensing Requirements” section of the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
.

### 6.3. The W3C Recommendation Track[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#rec-track)

[Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 create specifications and guidelines to complete the scope of work envisioned by a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
's [charter](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#charter)
. These [technical reports](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 undergo cycles of revision and review as they advance towards [W3C Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 status. Once review suggests the Working Group has met their requirements for a new standard, including [wide review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dfn-wide-review)
, a [Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 phase allows the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 to formally collect [implementation experience](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#implementation-experience)
 to demonstrate that the specification works in practice. At the end of the process, the Advisory Committee reviews the mature technical report, and if there is support from its Membership, W3C publishes it as a [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
.

In summary, the W3C Recommendation Track consists of:

1.  Publication of the [First Public Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#fpwd)
    .
2.  Publication of zero or more revised [Working Drafts](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsWD)
    .
3.  Publication of one or more [Candidate Recommendations](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
    .
4.  Publication of a [Proposed Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsPR)
    .
5.  Publication as a [W3C Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
    .

Basic W3C Recommendation Track First Public Working Draft (FPWD) - Exclusion opportunity First Public WD WG Decision + Team Approval Working Draft (WD) WD Publish a new Working Draft WG Decision Advance to Candidate Recommendation WG Decision + Team Approval Candidate Recommendation Snapshot (CRS) - Patent Policy exclusion opportunity CRS Candidate Recommendation Draft (CRD) CRD Publish revised Candidate Recommendation Draft WG Decision Publish revised Candidate Recommendation Draft WG Decision Publish revised Candidate Recommendation Snapshot WG Decision + Team Approval Publish revised Candidate Recommendation Snapshot WG Decision Team Approval Advance to Proposed Recommendation WG Decision + Team Approval Return to Working Draft WG Decision (or Team Decision with AB+TAG Approval) Proposed Recommendation (PR) — AC Review PR AC Review Advance to Recommendation W3C Decision Return to Candidate Recommendation Snapshot W3C Decision Return to Working Draft W3C Decision Recommendation (Rec) REC

This Process defines certain [Recommendation Track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-recommendation-track)
 publications as Patent Review Drafts. Under the 2004 Patent Policy (and its 2017 update) [\[PATENT-POLICY-2004\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy-2004 "The W3C 2004 Patent Policy, Updated 2017")
, these correspond to “Last Call Working Draft” in the Patent Policy; Starting from the 2020 Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY-2020\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy-2020 "The W3C 2020 Patent Policy")
, these correspond to “Patent Review Draft” in the Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
.

W3C _may_ [end work on a technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#tr-end)
 at any time.

As described in [§ 6.3.3 Advancement on the Recommendation Track](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#transition-reqs)
, the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 will decline a request to advance in maturity stage and return the specification to a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 for further work if it determines that the requirements for advancement have not been met.

#### 6.3.1. Maturity Stages on the Recommendation Track[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#maturity-stages)

Working Draft (WD)

A Working Draft is a document that W3C has [published](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#publishing)
 on the [W3C’s Technical Reports page](https://www.w3.org/TR/)
 [\[TR\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-tr "The W3C technical reports index")
 for review by the community (including W3C Members), the public, and other technical organizations, and for simple historical reference. Some, but not all, Working Drafts are meant to advance to [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
; see the [document status section](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#DocumentStatus)
 of a Working Draft for the group’s expectations. [Working Drafts](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsWD)
 do not necessarily represent a [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
 of the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 with respect to their content, and do not imply any endorsement by W3C or its members beyond agreement to work on a general area of technology. Nevertheless the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 decided to adopt the [Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsWD)
 as the basis for their work at the time of adoption. A [Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsWD)
 is suitable for gathering [wide review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dfn-wide-review)
 prior to advancing to the next stage of maturity.

For all Working Drafts a Working Group:

*   _should_ document outstanding issues, and parts of the document on which the Working Group does not have consensus, and
*   _may_ request publication of a Working Draft even if its content is considered unstable and does not meet all Working Group requirements.

The first Working Draft of a technical report is called the First Public Working Draft (FPWD), and has patent implications as defined in the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
.

Candidate Recommendation (CR)

A Candidate Recommendation is a document that satisfies the technical requirements of the Working Group that produced it and their dependencies, and has already received wide review. W3C publishes a Candidate Recommendation to

*   signal to the wider community that it is time to do a final review
*   gather [implementation experience](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#implementation-experience)
    

Note: Advancing to [Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 indicates that the document is considered complete and fit for purpose, and that no further refinement to the text is expected without additional implementation experience and testing; additional features in a later revision may however be expected. A [Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 is expected to be as well-written, detailed, self-consistent, and technically complete as a [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
, and acceptable as such if and when the requirements for further advancement are met.

Candidate Recommendation publications take one of two forms:

Candidate Recommendation Snapshot (CRS)

A Candidate Recommendation Snapshot corresponds to a [Patent Review Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#patent-review-drafts)
 as used in the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
. Publishing a [Patent Review Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#patent-review-drafts)
 triggers a Call for Exclusions, per “Exclusion From W3C RF Licensing Requirements” in the W3C Patent Policy.

Publication as a [Candidate Recommendation Snapshot](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-recommendation-snapshot)
 requires verification of either a [Transition Request](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#trans-req)
 (for the first [Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 publication from another maturity stage) or an [Update Request](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#update-requests)
 (for subsequent [Candidate Recommendation Snapshots](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-recommendation-snapshot)
).

Candidate Recommendation Draft (CRD)

A Candidate Recommendation Draft is published on the [W3C’s Technical Reports page](https://www.w3.org/TR/)
 [\[TR\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-tr "The W3C technical reports index")
 to integrate changes from the previous [Candidate Recommendation Snapshot](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-recommendation-snapshot)
 that the Working Group intends to include in a subsequent [Candidate Recommendation Snapshot](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-recommendation-snapshot)
. This allows for wider review of the changes and for ease of reference to the integrated specification.

Any changes published directly into a [Candidate Recommendation Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-recommendation-draft)
 should be at the same level of quality as a [Candidate Recommendation Snapshot](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-recommendation-snapshot)
. However, the process requirements are minimized so that the Working Group can easily keep the specification up to date.

A [Candidate Recommendation Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#candidate-recommendation-draft)
 _does not_ provide an exclusion opportunity; instead, it is considered a [Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsWD)
 for the purpose of the [W3C Patent Policy](https://www.w3.org/policies/patent-policy)
 [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
.

A Rescinded Candidate Recommendation is a [Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 in which significant problems have been discovered such that W3C cannot endorse it or continue work on it, for example due to burdensome patent claims that affect implementers and cannot be resolved (see the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
 and in particular “PAG Conclusion”). There is no path to restoration for a [Rescinded Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#rescinded-candidate-recommendation)
. See “W3C Royalty-Free (RF) Licensing Requirements” in the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
 for implication on patent licensing obligations.

Proposed Recommendation (PR)

A Proposed Recommendation is a document that has been accepted by W3C as of sufficient quality to become a [W3C Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
. This phase triggers formal review by the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
, who _may_ recommend that the document be [published](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#publishing)
 as a [W3C Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
, returned to the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 for further work, or abandoned. [Substantive changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#substantive-change)
 _must not_ be made to a [Proposed Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsPR)
 except by [publishing](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#publishing)
 a new [Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsWD)
 or [Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
.

W3C Recommendation (REC)

A W3C Recommendation is a specification or set of guidelines or requirements that, after extensive [consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
\-building, has received the endorsement of W3C and its Members. W3C recommends the wide deployment of its Recommendations as standards for the Web. The W3C Royalty-Free IPR licenses granted under the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
 apply to [W3C Recommendations](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
. As technology evolves, a [W3C Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 may become:

A Superseded Recommendation

A Superseded Recommendation is a specification that has been replaced by a newer version that W3C recommends for new adoption. An [Obsolete](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsObs)
 or Superseded specification has the same status as a [W3C Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 with regards to W3C Royalty-Free IPR Licenses granted under the Patent Policy.

Note: When a Technical Report which had previously been published as a [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 is again published as a [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 after following the necessary steps to [revise](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#revising-rec)
 it, the latest version replaces the previous one, without the need to invoke the steps of [§ 6.3.13.3 Abandoning a W3C Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#rec-rescind)
: it is the same document, updated. Explicitly declaring a documented superseded, using the process documented in [§ 6.3.13.3 Abandoning a W3C Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#rec-rescind)
, is intended for cases where a [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 is superseded by a separate [Technical Report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 (or by a document managed outside of W3C).

An Obsolete Recommendation

An Obsolete Recommendation is a specification that W3C has determined lacks sufficient market relevance to continue recommending it for implementation, but which does not have fundamental problems that would require it to be [Rescinded](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RescindedRec)
. If an Obsolete specification gains sufficient market relevance, W3C may decide to restore it to [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 status.

Rescinded Recommendation

A Rescinded Recommendation is an entire Recommendation that W3C no longer endorses, and believes is unlikely to ever be restored to [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 status. See also “W3C Royalty-Free (RF) Licensing Requirements” in the W3C Patent Policy [\[PATENT-POLICY\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-patent-policy "The W3C Patent Policy")
.

Discontinued Draft

A [technical report](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#technical-report)
 representing the state of a Recommendation-track document at the point at which work on it was discontinued. See [§ 6.3.13.1 Abandoning an Unfinished Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#abandon-draft)
.

Only sufficiently technically mature work should be advanced.

Note: Should faster advancement to meet scheduling considerations be desired, this can be achieved by reducing the scope of the technical report to a subset that is adequately mature and deferring less stable features to other technical reports.

When publishing an updated version of an existing [Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 or [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
, technical reports are expected to meet the same maturity criteria as when they are first published under that status. However, in the interest of replacing stale documents with improved ones in a timely manner, if flaws have been discovered in the technical report after its initial publication as a [CR](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 or [REC](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 that would have been severe enough to reject that publication had they be known in time, it is also permissible to publish an updated [CR](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 or [REC](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 following the usual process, even if only some of these flaws have been satisfactorily addressed.

[Working Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 and [Interest Groups](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsIG)
 _may_ make available [Editor’s drafts](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#editors-draft)
. Editor’s drafts (ED) have no official standing whatsoever, and do not necessarily imply consensus of a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 or [Interest Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsIG)
, nor are their contents endorsed in any way by W3C.

#### 6.3.2. Implementation Experience[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#implementation-experience)

Implementation experience is required to show that a specification is sufficiently clear, complete, and relevant to market needs, to ensure that independent interoperable implementations of each feature of the specification will be realized. While no exhaustive list of requirements is provided here, when assessing that there is adequate implementation experience the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 will consider (though not be limited to):

*   is each feature of the current specification implemented, and how is this demonstrated?
*   are there independent interoperable implementations of the current specification?
*   are there implementations created by people other than the authors of the specification?
*   are implementations publicly deployed?
*   is there implementation experience at all levels of the specification’s ecosystem (authoring, consuming, publishing…)?
*   are there reports of difficulties or problems with implementation?

Planning and accomplishing a demonstration of (interoperable) implementations can be very time consuming. Groups are often able to work more effectively if they plan how they will demonstrate interoperable implementations early in the development process; for example, developing tests in concert with implementation efforts.

#### 6.3.3. Advancement on the Recommendation Track[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#transition-reqs)

For _all_ requests to advance a specification to a new maturity stage (called Transition Requests), the Working Group:

*   _must_ record the group’s decision to request advancement.
*   _must_ obtain [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
     verification. [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
     verification (a [Team decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team-decision)
    ) _must_ be withheld if any Process requirements are not met or if there remain any unresolved Formal Objections (including any [upheld](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#uphold)
     by a [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
     but not yet [fully addressed](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#fully-addressed)
    ), or if the document does not adequately reflect all relevant decisions of the [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
     (or its delegates). If the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
     rejects a [Transition Request](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#trans-req)
     it _must_ indicate its rationale to the [Advisory Committee](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
     and the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
    .
*   _must_ publicly document all new features ([class 4 changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#correction-classes)
    ) to the technical report since the previous publication.
*   _must_ publicly document if other substantive changes ([class 3 changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#correction-classes)
    ) have been made, and _should_ document the details of such changes.
*   _should_ publicly document if [editorial changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#editorial-change)
     have been made, and _may_ document the details of such changes.
*   _must_ [formally address](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#formally-addressed)
     all issues raised about the document since the previous [maturity stage](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#maturity-stages)
    .
*   _must_ provide public documentation of any [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
    .
*   _should_ report which, if any, of the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
    's requirements for this document have changed since the previous step.
*   _should_ report any changes in dependencies with other groups.
*   _should_ provide information about implementations known to the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
    .

For a [First Public Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#fpwd)
 there is no “previous maturity stage”, so many requirements do not apply, and verification is normally fairly straightforward. For later stages, especially transition to [Candidate](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 or [Proposed Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsPR)
, there is usually a formal review meeting to verify that the requirements have been met.

[Transition Requests](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#trans-req)
 to [First Public Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#fpwd)
 or [Candidate Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsCR)
 will not normally be approved while a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
's [charter](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#charter)
 is undergoing or awaiting a decision on an [Advisory Committee Review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee-review)
.

#### 6.3.4. Updating Mature Publications on the Recommendation Track[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#update-reqs)

Certain requests to re-publish a specification within its current maturity stage (called Update Requests) require extra verification. For such [update requests](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#update-requests)
, the Working Group:

*   _must_ record the group’s decision to request the update.
*   _must_ show that the changes have received [wide review](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#dfn-wide-review)
    .
*   _must_ obtain [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
     verification, or fulfill the criteria for [§ 6.3.4.1 Streamlined Publication Approval](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#streamlined-update)
    . [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
     verification (a [Team decision](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team-decision)
    ), _should_ be withheld if any Process requirements are not met, and _may_ be withheld in consideration of unresolved Formal Objections (including any [upheld](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#uphold)
     by a [Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
     but not yet [fully addressed](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#fully-addressed)
    ) or if the document does not adequately reflect all relevant decisions of the [W3C Council](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#w3c-council)
     (or its delegates). If the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
     rejects an [Update Request](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#update-requests)
    , it must indicate its rationale to the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
    . If it waives any Process requirements, it must indicate its rationale to the [AC](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#advisory-committee)
    .
*   _must_ provide public documentation of any [Formal Objections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
    .
*   _must_ publicly document of all new features ([class 4 changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#correction-classes)
    ) to the technical report since the previous publication.
*   _must_ publicly document if other substantive changes ([class 3 changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#correction-classes)
    ) have been made, and _should_ document the details of such changes.
*   _should_ publicly document if [editorial changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#editorial-change)
     changes have been made, and _may_ document the details of such changes.
*   _must_ show that the revised specification meets all [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
     requirements, or explain why the requirements have changed or been deferred,
*   _should_ report which, if any, of the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
    's requirements for this document have changed since the previous step.
*   _should_ report any changes in dependencies with other groups.
*   _should_ provide information about implementations known to the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
    .

There is usually a formal review meeting to verify that the requirements have been met.

Note: [Update request](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#update-requests)
 verification is expected to be fairly simple compared to verification of a [transition request](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#trans-req)
.

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ announce the publication of the revised specification to other W3C groups and the Public.

##### 6.3.4.1. Streamlined Publication Approval[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#streamlined-update)

Note: These criteria are intentionally stricter than the general requirements for an [update request](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#update-requests)
. This is in order to minimize ambiguities and the need for expert judgment, and to make self-evaluation practical.

In order to streamline the publication process in non-controversial cases, verification of an [update request](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#update-requests)
 is automatically granted without formal review when the following _additional_ criteria are fulfilled:

*   There _must_ have been no changes to [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
     requirements about this document.
*   For each of the [W3C Horizontal Groups](https://www.w3.org/Guide/process/charter.html#horizontal-review)
     [\[CHARTER\]](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#biblio-charter "How to Create a Working Group or Interest Group")
    , if the Horizontal Review Group has made available a set criteria under which their review is not necessary, the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
     _must_ document that these criteria have been fulfilled. Otherwise, the [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
     _must_ show that review from that group has been solicited and received.
*   No [Formal Objection](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#FormalObjection)
     has been registered against the document.
*   The [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
     _must_ have [formally addressed](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#formally-addressed)
    :
    
    *   all issues raised against the document that resulted in changes since the previous publication
        
    *   all issues raised against changes since the previous publication
        
    *   all issues raised against the document that were closed since the previous publication with no change to the document
        
    
    The response to each of these issues _must_ be to the satisfaction of the person who raised it: their proposal has been accepted, or a compromise has been found, or they accepted the Working Group’s rationale for rejecting it.
    
    Note: This is stricter than the general Transition Request criteria.
    

Additionally, for updates to [Recommendations](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
 with [substantive changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#revised-rec-substantive)
 or [with new features](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#revised-rec-features)
:

*   Changes to the document are limited to [proposed corrections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#proposed-corrections)
     that were included in a [Last Call for Review of Proposed Corrections](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#last-call-for-review-of-proposed-corrections)
     possibly combined with [class 1 or 2 changes](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#correction-classes)
    , and/or (in the case of a [Recommendation](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsW3C)
     that [allows new features](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#allow-new-features)
    ) [proposed additions](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#proposed-additions)
     that were included in a [Last Call for Review of Proposed Additions](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#last-call-for-review-of-proposed-additions)
    .
*   The [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
     _must_ show that all changes have been implemented in at least 2 distinct products by 2 different implementers, as evidenced by passing tests of a test suite providing extensive coverage of the changes, or an alternative streamlined verification implementation requirement described in the [working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
     charter has been met.
    
    Note: This is stricter than the general criteria for [adequate implementation experience](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#adequate-implementation)
    .
    

The [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 must provide written evidence for these claims, and the [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 must make these answers publicly and permanently available.

After publication, if an AC Representative or Team member doubts that the evidence presented supports the claims, they _may_ request that a formal review meeting be convened post facto. If that review finds that the requirements were not fulfilled, the Team _may_ revert the changes by updating in place the status section to indicate that it has been reverted, and by republishing the previously approved version of the technical report.

#### 6.3.5. Publishing a First Public Working Draft[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#first-wd)

To publish the [First Public Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#fpwd)
 of a document, a [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 _must_ meet the applicable [requirements for advancement](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#transition-reqs)
.

The [Team](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#team)
 _must_ announce the publication of a [First Public Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#fpwd)
 to other W3C groups and to the public.

#### 6.3.6. Revising a Working Draft[](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#revising-wd)

A [Working Group](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#GroupsWG)
 _should_ [publish](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#publishing)
 a [Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsWD)
 to the W3C Technical Reports page when there have been significant changes to the previous published document that would benefit from review beyond the Working Group.

If 6 months elapse without significant changes to a specification, a Working Group _should_ publish a revised [Working Draft](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#RecsWD)
, whose status section _should_ indicate reasons for the lack of change.

To publish a revision of a Working draft, a Working Group:

*   _must_ record the group’s decision to request publication. [Consensus](https://www.w3.org/policies/process/20231103/#def-Consensus)
     is not required, as this is a procedural step,
